title: Efficient reasoning in PAC semantics
speaker: Brendan Juba
abstract: Machine learning is often employed as one step in a larger application, serving to perform information extraction or data mining for example. The rules obtained by such learning are then used as inputs to a further analysis. As a consequence of the inputs to this analysis having been learned, however, its conclusions can only be theoretically guaranteed to satisfy the same standard as the inputs---that of "PAC Semantics" (Valiant, 2000).

proof_stages.label: PAC-learning versus logical-validity conflict
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates PAC semantics by showing that statistically learned rules can be useful for reasoning while still failing to be valid in the ordinary Tarski sense.

proof_stages.label: Weak distributional PAC-validity semantics
proof_stages.summary: After contrasting richer probability and graphical-model logics, the speaker defines a formula to be one-minus-epsilon valid under a distribution when it is satisfied with that probability, allowing rules of thumb and union-bound reasoning over imperfect background knowledge.

proof_stages.label: Query-directed reasoning problem under incomplete information
proof_stages.summary: The two-stage pipeline of mining rules and then proving from them is replaced by a direct procedure taking examples and a query, with the goal of being complete for all useful premises that could have been verified from the data, especially when relevant attributes are missing.

proof_stages.label: Masking-process model of partial examples
proof_stages.summary: Partial information is modeled by drawing complete assignments from the underlying distribution and then applying random masking functions, while semantic validity remains measured on the unmasked complete distribution.

proof_stages.label: Restriction-based simulation for natural tractable proof fragments
proof_stages.summary: For resolution and other natural tractable fragments, the speaker defines testable premises via syntactic simplification under masks and proves a promise-problem algorithm by restricting the whole proof on each sample, using soundness and completeness to match reasoning from any testable premise.

proof_stages.label: Affine/parity setting and hardness of removing the promise
proof_stages.summary: To ask whether PAC-semantics reasoning can be easier than classical reasoning, the talk specializes to affine distributions arising from parity constraints, recalls hardness results without a proof/testability promise, and states the target quasi-polynomial improvement for biased masking.

proof_stages.label: Bias-gap mechanism and narrow-clause reconstruction
proof_stages.summary: Using the bias-gap property, wide clauses are either killed by random restrictions or contain narrow implied clauses whose polarities can be learned and used to delete literals, yielding a logarithmic-width refutation search and an extension to polynomial-calculus resolution.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and open problems
proof_stages.summary: The talk concludes that PAC semantics captures learnable rules of thumb at no extra cost for tractable fragments and sometimes with lower complexity, then asks whether useful premises can be found explicitly and whether the distributional and masking assumptions can be weakened.

examples.name: Aviary feeding log and flying birds
examples.note: The toy bird-feeding data set shows how a statistically supported rule such as 'birds did not eat fish' can combine with background rules to infer flight only in a high-probability, not Tarski-valid, sense.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the aviary log and bird rules to infer that birds do not eat fish and hence fly, and later reasons via a union bound about rare emus.

examples.name: Boolean encoding of bird species
examples.note: In response to a question, categorical attributes such as penguin/emu/finch are encoded by extended literals and mutual-exclusion constraints to show how the propositional setup can represent non-Boolean data.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker concretely describes encoding bird species as Boolean literals with mutual-exclusion constraints and exactly one species variable true.

examples.name: DNF counterexample to semantic tautology testing
examples.note: The DNF example clarifies that testability is defined by local syntactic simplification to true under a restriction, not by solving the generally harder semantic tautology problem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through how a 3-DNF behaves under local restriction simplification and why this differs from tautology testing.

passes.reason: ordinary DNF formula/class is explicitly used to show local syntactic simplification need not capture semantic tautology testing
